topics.title: What is Mobile Security?
topics.definition: Mobile Security is the practice of protecting smartphones, tablets, and other mobile devices from unauthorized access, malware, data theft, network attacks, and application vulnerabilities.

topics.title: 1. Android Architecture
topics.definition: Android is an open-source mobile operating system developed by Google based on the Linux Kernel.
examPoints: Android Architecture consists of five layers.

topics.title: Layer 1 — Applications
topics.definition: Contains all Android applications.
examPoints: Third-party applications are also installed here.

topics.title: Layer 2 — Application Framework
topics.definition: Provides APIs for application development.
examPoints: Applications communicate with hardware through this layer.

topics.title: Layer 3 — Android Runtime (ART)
topics.definition: ART executes Android applications.
examPoints: Older Android versions used Dalvik VM.

topics.title: Native Libraries
topics.definition: Written mostly in C/C++.
examPoints: Applications access these libraries through the framework.

topics.title: Layer 4 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)
topics.definition: Provides a standard interface between Android Framework and hardware.
examPoints: Applications never communicate directly with hardware.

topics.title: Layer 5 — Linux Kernel
topics.definition: Most important security layer.
examPoints: Android sandboxing is enforced by the Linux Kernel.

topics.definition: Android stores different files in different directories.

topics.title: Content Provider
topics.definition: Shares data between applications.

topics.title: Intent
topics.definition: Used for communication between Android components.

topics.title: Verified Boot
topics.definition: Checks operating system integrity during startup.

topics.title: SELinux
topics.definition: Mandatory Access Control.
examPoints: Restricts application capabilities even after compromise.

topics.definition: Rooting provides administrator (root) privileges.

topics.title: Exam Points
examPoints: Android security is enforced mainly by the Linux Kernel.
Every Android app runs inside its own sandbox.
AndroidManifest.xml stores permissions and component information.
ADB is used for debugging and device management.
MobSF performs Static and Dynamic analysis.
MITM intercepts communication between two parties.
Static analysis examines code without execution.

topics.title: Last-Minute Revision
topics.definition: # Mobile Security Notes
workflows: OWASP Mobile Top 10 = Common Mobile Vulnerabilities

topics.title: 1. Introduction to Mobile Security
topics.definition: Mobile security is the protection of smartphones, tablets, mobile applications, operating systems, stored data, and wireless communications from unauthorized access, malware, data leakage, and network attacks.
examPoints: Mobile devices are high-value targets because they commonly contain:

topics.title: 2. Android Architecture
topics.definition: Android uses a layered architecture.

topics.title: 2.1 Applications Layer
topics.definition: This layer contains:

topics.title: 2.2 Application Framework
topics.definition: The Application Framework provides high-level APIs and system services to applications.
examPoints: Applications use these services instead of directly interacting with the hardware.

topics.title: 2.3 Android Runtime
topics.definition: Modern Android uses ART, or Android Runtime.
examPoints: Older Android versions used the Dalvik Virtual Machine.

topics.title: 2.4 Native Libraries
topics.definition: Android includes native libraries written mainly in C and C++.

topics.title: 2.5 Hardware Abstraction Layer
topics.definition: The HAL provides a standard interface between Android software and device hardware.

topics.title: 2.6 Linux Kernel
topics.definition: The Linux Kernel is the foundation of Android.
examPoints: Android application isolation is mainly enforced through the Linux Kernel using separate Linux user IDs.

topics.title: 3. Android File Structure
topics.definition: Important Android directories include:

topics.title: /data/app
topics.definition: Contains installed application packages.

topics.title: /data/data
topics.definition: Contains private application data such as:
examPoints: Each application normally accesses only its own private directory.

topics.title: /sdcard
topics.definition: Contains shared user files.
examPoints: Sensitive information should not be stored here without protection because multiple applications may access shared storage depending on permissions and Android version.

topics.title: 4. Android Build Process
topics.definition: The Android build process converts source code and resources into an installable APK.

topics.title: Main steps
examPoints: Developer writes Java or Kotlin code.
Bytecode is converted into DEX format.
Files are packaged into an APK.
Android verifies and installs the application.

topics.title: 5. APK Structure
topics.definition: APK stands for Android Package.
examPoints: An APK is essentially an archive containing application code, resources, configuration, and signatures.

topics.title: 6. Android App Fundamentals
topics.definition: Android applications are built using four major components.

topics.title: 6.1 Activity
topics.definition: An Activity represents a user-interface screen.

topics.title: 6.2 Service
topics.definition: A Service performs background operations.

topics.title: 6.3 Broadcast Receiver
topics.definition: A Broadcast Receiver reacts to system or application events.

topics.title: 6.4 Content Provider
topics.definition: A Content Provider manages and shares structured data between applications.

topics.title: 7. Intent
topics.definition: An Intent is a messaging object used for communication between Android components.

topics.title: Explicit Intent
topics.definition: Specifies the exact target component.

topics.title: Implicit Intent
topics.definition: Describes an action and allows Android to find a suitable component.

topics.title: 8. AndroidManifest.xml
topics.definition: The Android Manifest is the main configuration file of an Android application.
examPoints: Analysts inspect the Manifest to identify:

topics.title: 9. Android Security Model
topics.definition: Android uses multiple security layers.

topics.title: 9.1 Application Sandbox
topics.definition: Each application normally receives:
examPoints: This prevents one application from directly accessing another application's private data.

topics.title: 9.2 Permission Model
topics.definition: Applications request permissions for sensitive operations.
examPoints: Modern Android versions require runtime approval for many dangerous permissions.
Applications should request only the permissions required for their functionality.

topics.title: 9.3 Application Signing
topics.definition: Every Android application must be signed.
examPoints: An update must normally be signed using the same signing key as the installed application.

topics.title: 9.4 SELinux
topics.definition: SELinux provides Mandatory Access Control.
examPoints: SELinux adds protection even if a process is compromised.

topics.title: 9.5 Verified Boot
topics.definition: Verified Boot checks the integrity of system components during startup.
examPoints: It helps detect unauthorized modification of the operating system.

topics.title: 10. Device Rooting
topics.definition: Rooting gives the user or an application root-level privileges on Android.

topics.title: Security risks
topics.definition: Rooting does not automatically infect a device, but it significantly increases the impact of a successful compromise.

topics.title: 11. Android Emulator
topics.definition: An Android Emulator is a virtual Android device running on a computer.

topics.title: Advantages
examPoints: No risk to a personal device
Multiple Android versions can be tested

topics.title: Limitations
examPoints: Certain sensors may not behave realistically

topics.title: 12. Android Debug Bridge
topics.definition: ADB stands for Android Debug Bridge.
examPoints: It allows communication between a computer and an Android device or emulator.

topics.title: Security risks of ADB
topics.definition: ADB should not be exposed unnecessarily over a network.

topics.title: 13. Mobile Penetration Testing
topics.definition: Mobile penetration testing evaluates the security of:

topics.title: 14. Static Analysis
topics.definition: Static analysis examines the application without running it.

topics.title: Limitations
examPoints: Dynamic code loading may be missed
Some findings may be false positives

topics.title: 15. Dynamic Analysis
topics.definition: Dynamic analysis examines an application while it is running.

topics.title: Limitations
examPoints: Some functionality may require real accounts

topics.title: 16. Static vs Dynamic Analysis
comparisons: Application is not executed: Application is executed
Examines APK and code: Examines runtime behaviour
Finds hardcoded secrets: Finds live network and API activity
Uses JADX, APKTool, MobSF: Uses Frida, ADB, MobSF

topics.title: 17. APKTool
topics.definition: APKTool is used to decode and rebuild Android APK files.

topics.title: APKTool output
topics.definition: APKTool is more suitable for APK structure and resource analysis than Java source recovery.

topics.title: 18. JADX
topics.definition: JADX decompiles DEX bytecode into readable Java-like source code.

topics.title: Limitation
topics.definition: The recovered Java source may not exactly match the original source code.
examPoints: Obfuscation can make the output difficult to read.

topics.title: 19. APKTool vs JADX
comparisons: Decodes APK resources: Decompiles DEX code
Produces smali code: Produces Java-like code
Useful for rebuilding: Useful for code review
Examines Manifest and resources: Examines application logic

topics.title: 20. Frida
topics.definition: Frida is a dynamic instrumentation framework.
examPoints: It allows an analyst to hook functions while the application is running.

topics.title: Security significance
topics.definition: Frida demonstrates that sensitive security checks should not depend entirely on client-side logic.

topics.title: 21. Reverse Engineering
topics.definition: Reverse engineering is the process of understanding an application without access to its original source code.

topics.title: 22. MobSF
topics.definition: MobSF stands for Mobile Security Framework.
examPoints: It supports automated mobile application analysis.

topics.title: MobSF advantages
examPoints: Useful for Android and other mobile platforms

topics.title: Limitation
topics.definition: MobSF results must be manually verified.

topics.title: 23. OWASP Mobile Top 10
topics.definition: The OWASP Mobile Top 10 describes major mobile-application security risks.
examPoints: Terminology varies between editions, but the core security areas include the following.

topics.title: 23.1 Improper Platform Usage
topics.definition: The application incorrectly uses platform security features.

topics.title: 23.2 Insecure Data Storage
topics.definition: Sensitive data is stored without sufficient protection.

topics.title: 23.3 Insecure Communication
topics.definition: Sensitive information is transmitted insecurely.

topics.title: 23.4 Insecure Authentication
topics.definition: The application incorrectly verifies user identity.

topics.title: 23.5 Insecure Authorization
topics.definition: The application fails to verify permissions for each action.
examPoints: One user accesses another user's data

topics.title: 23.6 Insufficient Cryptography
topics.definition: The application uses weak or incorrectly implemented cryptography.
examPoints: MD5 or SHA-1 for security-sensitive purposes

topics.title: 23.7 Client Code Quality
topics.definition: Poor coding practices create vulnerabilities.

topics.title: 23.8 Code Tampering
topics.definition: Attackers modify and repackage an APK.

topics.title: 23.9 Reverse Engineering
topics.definition: Attackers analyze the application to understand:
examPoints: No client-side code can be made completely impossible to reverse engineer.

topics.title: 23.10 Extraneous Functionality
topics.definition: Production applications may contain unintended functionality.

topics.title: 24. Attacks on Android Applications
topics.definition: Common Android attacks include:

topics.title: 25. Exported Component Attacks
topics.definition: An exported component may be accessible by other applications.

topics.title: 26. WebView Security
topics.definition: WebView displays web content inside an Android application.

topics.title: 27. Smishing
topics.definition: Smishing is phishing performed through SMS.

topics.title: 28. Phishing
topics.definition: Phishing tricks users into revealing sensitive information through fake websites, emails, or applications.

topics.title: Mobile phishing risks
examPoints: Mobile browsers show limited certificate information

topics.title: 29. MITM Attack
topics.definition: A Man-in-the-Middle attack occurs when an attacker secretly intercepts communication between two parties.

topics.title: 30. Packet Sniffing with Wireshark
topics.definition: Wireshark captures and analyzes network traffic.

topics.title: Information visible in unencrypted traffic
topics.definition: HTTPS payloads are encrypted, although metadata such as IP addresses and connection details may still be visible.

topics.title: 31. Mobile Malware
topics.definition: Mobile malware is malicious software designed to target mobile devices.

topics.title: 32. Android Malware Behaviour
topics.definition: Android malware may:

topics.title: 33. Banking Trojan
topics.definition: A banking Trojan targets financial applications.

topics.title: 34. Android Ransomware
topics.definition: Android ransomware may:

topics.title: 35. Android Malware Persistence
topics.definition: Persistence mechanisms may include:

topics.title: 36. Rooted Devices and Malware
topics.definition: On a rooted device, malware may gain:
examPoints: Rooting significantly weakens the trust boundary between applications.

topics.title: 38. Mobile Application Penetration Testing Areas
topics.definition: A complete test should examine:

topics.title: 39. Basic MobSF Lab Workflow
examPoints: Start MobSF in an isolated laboratory.

topics.title: Secure storage
examPoints: Avoid shared storage for sensitive information

topics.title: 41. Important Tool Comparison
comparisons: APKTool: Decode and rebuild APK resources
JADX: Decompile DEX into Java-like code
MobSF: Automated static and dynamic analysis
Burp Suite: Intercept and modify application traffic

topics.title: Rooting vs Malware
topics.definition: Rooting is not malware, but it can make malware more powerful.

topics.title: 43. Important Exam Points
oneLiners: The Linux Kernel provides process isolation and sandboxing.
Each application normally has a separate Linux UID.
ADB allows shell, install, push, pull, and logging operations.
MobSF performs automated mobile application analysis.
Static analysis does not execute the app.
Insecure Data Storage exposes local sensitive information.
Exported components may be abused by other applications.
Mobile malware may abuse Accessibility Services.
Automated findings must be manually verified.

topics.title: 44. Last-Minute Revision Sheet
workflows: Overlay → fake screen over genuine app
